<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">We are
invited to become God’s family.</span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">God’s
work in the world may not be recognized by many in the world, but some will
recognize that work, and those will be recognized by God.</span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">When God
wants to do his greatest work, he becomes one of the very humans he created to
show them what he is like.</span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .25in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level1 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: Wingdings; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-bidi-font-family: Wingdings; mso-fareast-font-family: Wingdings;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">Ø<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp; </span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">What does the passage teach us about the
responsibilities God gives to humankind?</span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">Just as
God works through his Logos, he also works through his people. Rather than
announcing his purposes from the sky, he begins by sending a Spirit-empowered
human in the form of John the Baptist. </span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">He gives
humans access to light and life; however, he will not force them to accept it. </span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">Just as
he will not force them to accept it, he will not provide them another avenue
either. As a parent might refuse to make a different meal than the one
prepared, the ending of the prologue makes it clear that no one can see God as
Father unless they come to him through his Logos. </span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .25in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level1 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: Wingdings; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-bidi-font-family: Wingdings; mso-fareast-font-family: Wingdings;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">Ø<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp; </span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">What does the passage teach us about how people
should treat each other, particularly in light of our relationship with God?</span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">While nothing directly speaks to human-to-human
relationships, there are inferences available in the text:</span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">If John
the Baptist and the Logos himself could come and not be recognized by the very
people who studied the “word” day and night, we should be cautious when we
speak to believers we disagree with. It may turn out to be that they are trying
to bring us the light that we need and we are too blind to see it. </span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpLast" style="line-height: normal; margin-left: .75in; mso-add-space: auto; mso-list: l2 level2 lfo4; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;; font-size: 12.0pt; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Courier New&quot;;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">o<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">Just as
Moses gave Torah (teachings) and the Logos expanded those teachings, we may
come to the things of God in stages. We should appreciate those who came
before, but not fail to move forward with the new things God wants to show us.
However, they should always align, as Torah/Logos do, in the beginning. </span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<h1><span style="mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">Lexicography</span></h1>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">This Lexicography is using reproduces but
condenses the Mounce Lexicon unless otherwise indicated.<a href="#_ftn23" name="_ftnref23" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn23;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[23]</span></span></span></span></a>
For brevity, some examples are included, but not an exhaustive list.</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .25in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">(794) ἀρχή <b>archē</b> 55x “a <b>beginning</b>”
(Mt. 24:8), can refer to an extremity or corner (Acts 10:11; 11:5), but typically
referring to authority and headship (Jude 6; Lk. 20:20; Lk. 12:11; Eph. 3:10;
6:12). Could also refer to “from the first” (Mt. 19:4, 8; Lk. 1:2; Jn. 6:64; 2
Thess. 2:13; 1 Jn. 1:1; 2:7) <b>or “in the beginning of things”</b> (<b>Jn. 1:1</b>,
2; Heb. 1:10, Acts 11:15). <span style="mso-tab-count: 1;"> </span></span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .25in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">(3364) λόγος <b>logos</b> 330x “a <b>word</b>”,
typically used in the common sense of “speech, language, talk”. However, could
also have conceptual links to the Hebrew concepts of “the Word of Yahweh” which
came to the prophets. (Mt. 13:21, 22; Mk. 16:20; Lk. 1:2; Acts 6:4); and in
only one place in the New Testament, it is used as <b>“the divine WORD” (or
Logos) (Jn. 1:1).</b> Originally a mathematical term referring to giving
account (bookkeeping), Logos came to have strong ties to Greek Philosophers
such as Pythagoras, Heraclitus, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, the Stoics, and the
Hebrew-Greek Philosopher Philo.<a href="#_ftn24" name="_ftnref24" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn24;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[24]</span></span></span></span></a></span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst" style="line-height: 200%; mso-list: l0 level1 lfo3; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: Symbol; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">·<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">See
also, the Old Testament, “dabar” Mounce expands here to say that while the word
“dabar/word” is common in the Hebrew for all sorts of speech, Human and God, it
is often used to refer to “The Dabar of Yahweh has come to me”. Verb: </span><span dir="RTL" lang="HE" style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">דָּבַר</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span> (dābar), GK 1819 (S 1696), 1136×; Noun: </span><span dir="RTL" lang="HE" style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">דָּבָר</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span> (dābār), GK 1821 (S 1697), 1454×; Noun: </span><span dir="RTL" lang="HE" style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">נְאֻם</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span>
(neʾum), GK 5536 (S 5002), 376×. neʾum.<a href="#_ftn25" name="_ftnref25" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn25;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[25]</span></span></span></span></a>
See also, </span><span dir="RTL" lang="HE" style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">קָרָא</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span lang="HE" style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span> </span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">(qara) vb. call, proclaim, read and </span><span dir="RTL" lang="HE" style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">אָמַר</span><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span lang="HE" style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span dir="LTR"></span><span dir="LTR"></span> </span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">(amar) vb.
utter, say from Genesis One.<a href="#_ftn26" name="_ftnref26" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn26;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[26]</span></span></span></span></a></span></p>

<p class="MsoListParagraphCxSpLast" style="line-height: 200%; mso-list: l0 level1 lfo3; text-indent: -.25in;"><span style="font-family: Symbol; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;"><span style="mso-list: Ignore;">·<span style="font: 7.0pt &quot;Times New Roman&quot;;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span></span><span dir="LTR"></span><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">This
“Word/Dabar”, Dr. Tim Mackie says, is seen as the subject of the verb in the vision
recorded in Genesis 15:1, and thus the “Word of Yahweh” becomes seen as an
analog for Yahweh himself in later Jewish traditions.<a href="#_ftn27" name="_ftnref27" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn27;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[27]</span></span></span></span></a>
This blends into the theme of God using his Logos at work in the world in Greek
thought, and the two are combined in the work of Philo.</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .25in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">(2437) ζωή zōē 135× <b>life, living existence (</b>Lk.
16:25; Acts 17:25) spiritual life (Jn. 6:51; Rom. 5:18; 6:4); the final life of
the redeemed, (Mt. 25:46) life, source of spiritual life. Aligned with the
Hebrew background for these concepts, this should be understood as related to
the Tree of Life in Genesis 2; and to the Resurrection of the Dead at the Great
Day of Yahweh, in which everyone loyal to Yahweh is brought back from the grave
and invited into the eschatological Kingdom of God. Life, in the mouths of
Greek-speaking Hebrew minds, refers to the life we have a taste of now and a
promise of in the age to come. For the Hebrew, the grave is not the end, a
redemption will come at the end. We will live, embodied, again. Thus, Paul goes
on to talk about a day when all believers will rise from the dead, and together
we will get new bodies. This concept is what is meant by “eternal (zoe) life”
when spoken by a Hebrew-minded writer.</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .25in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">(5890) φῶς phōs 73× <b>light </b>(Mt. 17:2; 2
Cor. 4:6) daylight, broad day (Mt. 10:27; Lk. 12:3) from the Hebrew, the light
of God’s presence (2 Cor. 11:14; 1 Tim. 6:16) the light of Divine truth,
spiritual illumination (Lk. 16:8; Jn. 3:19; Rom. 13:12; Eph. 5:8; 1 Pet. 2:9; 1
Jn. 1:7; 2:8, 9, 10). Contextualized by John, this word refers to the very
first act of creation, “and Elohim said ‘light be’ and light be’d”. It was
through the Logos that light and life were given to humankind, both in the
beginning and in this new beginning. </span></p>

<h1><span style="mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">Authorial
Intent (Message/Argument of the Text)</span></h1>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span style="mso-tab-count: 1;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>In the late first century AD, John
took his residence in Ephesus and became pastor over Asia Minor (hence his
seven letters in Revelation). Either John the Apostle or John the Elder working
with John the Apostle may have received a revelation from the Apostle Andrew to
write a fourth gospel account.<a href="#_ftn28" name="_ftnref28" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn28;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[28]</span></span></span></span></a> At
this point, the Synoptics, most epistles, and Paul’s letters had circulated
through the churches. Paul who began the work in Asia Minor was later killed by
the Romans, as was Peter. John built on Paul’s work in Ephesus. As an old man,
John had decades to reflect on the life of Jesus, speaking about it with anyone
who would listen. He had read the works of Matthew, Mark, and Luke and felt no
need to repeat their works. John is also fighting increased emperor worship in
his time, as Caesar is called “lord and god” by his people.<a href="#_ftn29" name="_ftnref29" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn29;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[29]</span></span></span></span></a></span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span style="mso-tab-count: 1;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>Bringing together his life as a
Hebrew fisherman in Galilee and his life as a world-traveler among the Greeks,
John sets out to show the world a more intimate and theologically themed
narrative about the life of the man who changed everything. He allows his words
to do triple time, relating Hebrew and Greek ideas with a narrative aimed
directly at the emperor worship of his day. Clement of Alexandria said that
John had seen all the basic facts laid out by the other writers and wanted to
compose a gospel aimed at the core theological themes of Jesus’ ministry, a
“spiritual gospel”.<a href="#_ftn30" name="_ftnref30" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn30;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[30]</span></span></span></span></a> </span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span style="mso-tab-count: 1;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>While recent scholarship has shown
the works of the Synoptics to demonstrate high-Christology once understood in
their own original Hebrew contexts,<a href="#_ftn31" name="_ftnref31" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn31;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[31]</span></span></span></span></a>
John’s gospel remains the clearest indication to lay-readers of his divinity
focusing on the unity of Father and Son.<a href="#_ftn32" name="_ftnref32" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn32;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[32]</span></span></span></span></a> To
begin this narrative, John chooses a framework that highlights Jesus as
God-incarnate. Mackie says that Jesus is shown in John’s prologue to be equal
with Yahweh, and then he spends the rest of the gospel showing Jesus going
around doing “Yahweh Stuff”.<a href="#_ftn33" name="_ftnref33" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn33;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[33]</span></span></span></span></a> Blum
summarizes: </span></p>

<p class="MsoQuote" style="margin-bottom: 0in;"><span style="mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">It is almost as if John had said, “I want you
to consider Jesus in His teaching and deeds. But you will not understand the
good news of Jesus in its fullest sense unless you view Him from this point of
view. Jesus is God manifest in the flesh, and His words and deeds are those of
the God-Man.”<a href="#_ftn34" name="_ftnref34" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn34;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><b style="mso-bidi-font-weight: normal;"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[34]</span></b></span></span></span></a></span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span style="mso-tab-count: 1;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>Building on the work of Philo, John
brings ancient Hebrew and new Greek ideas to bear on the theological
significance of the life of Jesus. This is no mere prophet or philosopher or
martyr. Jesus is the Logos the Greeks are searching to understand. He is the
manifested Word of God which was in operation throughout Yahweh’s work with
Israel. Even before anything existed, Jesus was there, as the Logos, the Word.
Jesus was not only with God, but he was also God. Separate and together. Many
in the Jewish community were already familiar with the Two Powers in Heaven and
some knew of Philo’s work showing Yahweh worked through his “son” the Logos,
now John was introducing these concepts in a new way, one that tied them
directly to this one man in real history and his impact on the world.<a href="#_ftn35" name="_ftnref35" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn35;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">[35]</span></span></span></span></a></span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span style="mso-tab-count: 1;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>Humanity was groping about in
darkness and death, and the light-giver and life-giver came to bring them hope.
However, many failed to see it for what it was. Even after a movement had
started and gospels were written and shared, there remained only a portion of
the population that believed the good news of the Yeshua (Iesus/Jesus) as the
real Lord (kurios) and God (theos) who had come to bring peace (counter to the
Pax Romana). These were all direct opposition to the claims of Caesar. John upped
the ante and demonstrated once and for all that one must choose Caesar or Jesus
as Lord. And yet, the words he chooses do not speak as one who lays down a
demand but as one pleading from compassion. </span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;"><span style="mso-tab-count: 1;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>John speaks of new creation, light,
life, and a new family of God. John centers his prologue around the invitation:
“as many as received him – to those who believe in his name – he gave them
authority to become children of God” (1:12, see bolded in the introduction to
this paper). Just as God once created a physical world and a physical human and
breathed life into that human; God was again at work in the world breathing new
life into humanity. As God had intended to walk with Adam in the cool of the
day, God was again calling humanity out to become children and family. The
prologue ends with the desire of John’s heart “to make Him known” just as the
Logos had made the heart of the father “known” (1:18). </span></p>

<h1><span style="mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">Conclusion</span></h1>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">After reviewing the text, the situational
background, and exploring the themes presented by John, this paper concludes
that John’s primary message both in his book and in this section (prologue) was
to demonstrate the Jesus was the Logos (the search of the Greek) and the Word
(the second power in heaven) who came to offer the opportunity to become
children of God. John wanted to “make him known” so that everyone would have
the opportunity to connect with God’s heart to have his human family return to
him. To do this, he spent his words repeating and driving home the theme that
this was no mere man, no mere prophet, but was the very Logos of Yahweh/God.
The rest of the book of John outlines the life of Jesus in ways that highlight
these points. The themes of the Logos at work in the world by bringing light
and life are repeated throughout the book. To everyone who believes, they are
given the opportunity to become God’s kids. Just as “the disciple whom Jesus
loved” was intimate with God’s heart, he desired everyone else should be too.
John knew that others had written the facts of Jesus, John wanted to
communicate the heart of Jesus and his Father to his lost and hurting humanity.
God wanted his family back. </span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 200%; text-indent: .5in;"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 200%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<h1><span style="mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">Bibliography</span></h1>

<p class="MsoNormal"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">“Bible
Translation Spectrum.” Accessed November 20, 2021.
https://wiki.logos.com/Bible_Translation_Spectrum.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>Christian Standard Bible® (CSB)</i>. Nashville,
Tennessee.: Holman Bible Pub, 2017.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Clendenen, E. Ray, and Jeremy Howard. <i>Holman
Illustrated Bible Commentary</i>. Broadman &amp; Holman, 2015.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">“Enuma Elish - New World Encyclopedia.” Accessed
November 17, 2021. https://www.newworldencyclopedia.org/entry/Enuma_Elish.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>&nbsp;</i></p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>Faithlife Study Bible, via Logos Software</i>.
Faithlife / Logos Bible Software, 2018.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Fee, Gordon D., and Robert L. Hubbard, eds. <i>The
Eerdmans Companion to the Bible</i>. Grand Rapids, Mich. ; Cambridge, U.K: W.B.
Eerdmans Pub. Co, 2011.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Fee, Gordon D., and Douglas K. Stuart. <i>How to Read
the Bible Book by Book: A Guided Tour</i>. Grand Rapids, Mich: Zondervan, 2002.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Gesenius, Wilhelm, Charles Briggs, S.R. Driver, and
Francis Brown. <i>The Abridged Brown-Driver-Briggs Hebrew-English Lexicon of
the Old Testament: From A Hebrew and English Lexicon of the Old Testament.</i>
Boston; New York: Houghton, Mifflin and Company, 1906.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Goodrich, Richard J, Albert L Lukaszewski, A. Philip
Brown, and Bryan W Smith. <i>A reader’s Hebrew and Greek Bible: 2nd edition.</i>,
2020.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Gore, Charles, Henry Leighton Goudge, and Alfred
Guillaume, eds. <i>A NEW COMMENTARY ON HOLY SCRIPTURE INCLUDING THE APOCRYPHA</i>.
Vol. 1, p. Iii. New York, NY: The Macmillan Company, 1928.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Jamieson, Robert, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown. <i>Commentary
Critical and Explanatory on the Whole Bible (1871)</i>. Oak Harbor, WA: Logos
Research Systems, Inc., 1997.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Mackie, Tim, and Jon Collins. “BibleProject<sup>TM</sup>
Videos and Podcasts.” Accessed June 18, 2021. https://bibleproject.com/.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">———. “God’s Word, Spirit, &amp; Wisdom Podcast |
BibleProject<sup>TM</sup>.” BibleProject. Accessed November 26, 2021.
https://bibleproject.com/podcast/theme-god-e11-gods-word-spirit-wisdom/.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Mounce, William D., ed. <i>Mounce’s Complete
Expository Dictionary of Old &amp; New Testament Words</i>. Grand Rapids, Mich:
Zondervan, 2006.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">“NJAB - Comparison Chart of Bible Translations Showing
Style or Type of Translation and Readability or Grade Level.” Accessed November
20, 2021. https://notjustanotherbook.com/biblecomparison.htm.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Plummer, Rob. <i>Daily Dose of Greek</i>. Daily Dose
of Greek. Louisville, KY: Daily Dose of Greek and Hebrew. Accessed November 27,
2021. https://dailydoseofgreek.com/about/.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Segal, Alan F. <i>Two Powers in Heaven: Early Rabbinic
Reports about Christianity and Gnosticism</i>. Studies in Judaism in Late Antiquity,
v. 25. Leiden: Brill, 1977.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Swete, Henry Barclay. <i>The Old Testament in Greek:
According to the Septuagint</i>. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press,
1909.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>&nbsp;</i></p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>The Lexham Bible Dictionary - Barry, J. D., Bomar,
D., Brown, D. R., Klippenstein, R., Mangum, D., Sinclair Wolcott, C., … Widder,
W. (Eds.). (2016). In The Lexham Bible Dictionary. Bellingham, WA: Lexham
Press.</i> Billingham, WA: Leham Press, 2016. LexhamPress.com.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Bible Bookshelf blog. “The Lexham English Bible: Dark
Horse of English Bible Versions.” Accessed November 20, 2021.
http://biblebookshelf.weebly.com/1/post/2017/01/the-lexham-english-bible-dark-horse-of-english-bible-versions.html.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>&nbsp;</i></p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>The Lexham English Bible (LEB), Fourth Edition</i>.
Logo Bible Software. Harris, W. H., III, Ritzema, E., Brannan, R., Mangum, D.,
Dunham, J., Reimer, J. A., &amp; Wierenga, M. (Eds.). Bellingham, WA: Lexham
Press, 2010. http://www.lexhampress.com.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>&nbsp;</i></p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>The Lexham Hebrew Bible (LHB)</i>. Bellingham, WA:
Leham Press, 2012.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>&nbsp;</i></p>

<p class="MsoBibliography"><i>The NET Bible First Edition Notes</i>. Biblical
Studies Press, 2006.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Walton, John H. <i>The Lost World of Genesis One:
Ancient Cosmology and the Origins Debate.</i> Westmont: InterVarsity Press,
2010.
https://public.ebookcentral.proquest.com/choice/publicfullrecord.aspx?p=2030851.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Walvoord, John F., Roy B. Zuck, and Dallas Theological
Seminary, eds. <i>The Bible Knowledge Commentary: An Exposition of the
Scriptures</i>. Wheaton, Ill: Victor Books, 1983.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Wolfe, Darrell. “Class Assignment: Situational
Reconstruction | Philemon.” Paper presented at the Biblical Background and
Interpretation (2021FA-BIBL-2301-ONL), The King’s University, Southlake, Texas,
November 4, 2021.
http://www.darrellwolfe.com/2021/11/class-assignment-situational.html.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Wright, N. T. <i>Jesus and the Victory of God</i>. 1st
North American ed. Vol. Vol 2. Christian Origins and the Question of God.
Minneapolis, MN: Fortress Press, 1996.</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">&nbsp;</p>

<p class="MsoBibliography">Wright, N. T., and Michael F. Bird. <i>The New
Testament in Its World: An Introduction to the History, Literature, and
Theology of the First Christians</i>. London : Grand Rapids, MI: SPCK ;
Zondervan Academic, 2019.</p>

<p class="MsoNormal"><span style="font-size: 12.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ascii-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-ascii-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-theme-font: major-bidi; mso-hansi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-hansi-theme-font: major-bidi;">&nbsp;</span></p>

<div style="mso-element: footnote-list;"><br clear="all" />

<hr align="left" size="1" width="33%" />



<div id="ftn1" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref1" name="_ftn1" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn1;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[1]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">“Bible
Translation Spectrum,” accessed November 20, 2021,
https://wiki.logos.com/Bible_Translation_Spectrum; “The Lexham English Bible:
Dark Horse of English Bible Versions,” Bible Bookshelf blog, accessed November
20, 2021,
http://biblebookshelf.weebly.com/1/post/2017/01/the-lexham-english-bible-dark-horse-of-english-bible-versions.html.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn2" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref2" name="_ftn2" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn2;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[2]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">The
Lexham English Bible (LEB), Fourth Edition</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">, Logo Bible Software, Harris,
W. H., III, Ritzema, E., Brannan, R., Mangum, D., Dunham, J., Reimer, J. A.,
&amp; Wierenga, M. (Eds.) (Bellingham, WA: Lexham Press, 2010), John 1:1–18,
http://www.lexhampress.com; “NJAB - Comparison Chart of Bible Translations
Showing Style or Type of Translation and Readability or Grade Level,” accessed
November 20, 2021, https://notjustanotherbook.com/biblecomparison.htm.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn3" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref3" name="_ftn3" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn3;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[3]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">The
Lexham Hebrew Bible (LHB)</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;"> (Bellingham, WA: Leham Press, 2012),
English translation of Gen 1:1 my own.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn4" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref4" name="_ftn4" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn4;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[4]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Rob
Plummer, <i>Daily Dose of Greek</i>, Daily Dose of Greek (Louisville, KY: Daily
Dose of Greek and Hebrew), John 1, accessed November 27, 2021,
https://dailydoseofgreek.com/about/; <i>LHB</i>, Genesis 1; Henry Barclay
Swete, <i>The Old Testament in Greek: According to the Septuagint</i>
(Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press, 1909), Genesis 1; Richard J
Goodrich et al., <i>A reader’s Hebrew and Greek Bible: 2nd edition.</i>, 2020,
John 1.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn5" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref5" name="_ftn5" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn5;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[5]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">The
NET Bible First Edition Notes</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;"> (Biblical Studies Press, 2006), Notes
for 1:1.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn6" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref6" name="_ftn6" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn6;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[6]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">The
NET Bible First Edition Notes</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">, Notes for 1:1.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn7" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref7" name="_ftn7" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn7;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[7]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">“Enuma
Elish - New World Encyclopedia,” accessed November 17, 2021,
https://www.newworldencyclopedia.org/entry/Enuma_Elish; John H Walton, <i>The
Lost World of Genesis One: Ancient Cosmology and the Origins Debate.</i>
(Westmont: InterVarsity Press, 2010),
https://public.ebookcentral.proquest.com/choice/publicfullrecord.aspx?p=2030851.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn8" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref8" name="_ftn8" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn8;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[8]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">The
Lexham Bible Dictionary - Barry, J. D., Bomar, D., Brown, D. R., Klippenstein,
R., Mangum, D., Sinclair Wolcott, C., … Widder, W. (Eds.). (2016). In The
Lexham Bible Dictionary. Bellingham, WA: Lexham Press.</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">
(Billingham, WA: Leham Press, 2016), o<span style="mso-tab-count: 1;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>Brian
K. Gamel, “Logos, Greek Background,” ed. John D. Barry et al., LexhamPress.com.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn9" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref9" name="_ftn9" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn9;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[9]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">William
D. Mounce, ed., <i>Mounce’s Complete Expository Dictionary of Old &amp; New
Testament Words</i> (Grand Rapids, Mich: Zondervan, 2006).</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn10" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref10" name="_ftn10" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn10;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[10]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Gordon
D. Fee and Robert L. Hubbard, eds., <i>The Eerdmans Companion to the Bible</i>
(Grand Rapids, Mich. ; Cambridge, U.K: W.B. Eerdmans Pub. Co, 2011), 585.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn11" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref11" name="_ftn11" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn11;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[11]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Charles
Gore, Henry Leighton Goudge, and Alfred Guillaume, eds., <i>A NEW COMMENTARY ON
HOLY SCRIPTURE INCLUDING THE APOCRYPHA</i>, Vol. 1, p. iii (New York, NY: The
Macmillan Company, 1928), W. Lock, “The Gospel according to St. John,.”</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn12" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref12" name="_ftn12" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn12;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[12]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Christian
Standard Bible® (CSB)</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;"> (Nashville, Tennessee.: Holman Bible
Pub, 2017), Andreas J. Köstenberger, “John,”; E. Ray Clendenen and Jeremy
Howard, <i>Holman Illustrated Bible Commentary</i> (Broadman &amp; Holman,
2015), Andreas Köstenberger, “John,”; Gordon D. Fee and Douglas K. Stuart, <i>How
to Read the Bible Book by Book: A Guided Tour</i> (Grand Rapids, Mich:
Zondervan, 2002), 304; Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown, <i>Commentary
Critical and Explanatory on the Whole Bible (1871)</i> (Oak Harbor, WA: Logos
Research Systems, Inc., 1997), 126.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn13" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref13" name="_ftn13" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn13;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[13]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Faithlife
Study Bible, via Logos Software</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;"> (Faithlife / Logos Bible Software,
2018), Introduction to John, John D. Barry et al.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn14" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref14" name="_ftn14" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn14;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[14]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Faithlife
Study Bible, via Logos Software</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">, Craig S. Keener, “The Gospel of John
and the Johannine Letters,”.</span> The Beloved Disciple holds a
place of special honor in John 13:23, and options for who occupied this
position appear limited (though some suggest Lazarus or Thomas). Besides John,
Jesus’ inner circle in the other first-century Gospels included only James, who
was martyred early (Acts 12:2), and Peter, whom John’s Gospel distinguishes
from the Beloved Disciple (John 13:24; 20:4–6)… Differing significantly from
the earlier Synoptic Gospels, the Gospel of John itself took time to gain wide
acceptance, which may suggest John the Elder as its author. However, the son of
Zebedee, by contrast, is clearly close to Jesus and this gives him probable
status as the Beloved Disciple.</p>

</div>

<div id="ftn15" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref15" name="_ftn15" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn15;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[15]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">N. T.
Wright and Michael F. Bird, <i>The New Testament in Its World: An Introduction
to the History, Literature, and Theology of the First Christians</i> (London :
Grand Rapids, MI: SPCK ; Zondervan Academic, 2019), 652–63.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn16" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref16" name="_ftn16" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn16;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[16]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">John
F. Walvoord, Roy B. Zuck, and Dallas Theological Seminary, eds., <i>The Bible
Knowledge Commentary: An Exposition of the Scriptures</i> (Wheaton, Ill: Victor
Books, 1983), Edwin A. Blum, “John,.”</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn17" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref17" name="_ftn17" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn17;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[17]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Fee
and Stuart, <i>How to Read the Bible Book by Book</i>, 411.</span> Note: John feels the need to
clarify that John the Baptist is not Jesus; however, never feels the need to
clarify that John the Baptist is not the same as John the Apostle. A possible
indication of John the Apostle being the author, audience familiarity.</p>

</div>

<div id="ftn18" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref18" name="_ftn18" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn18;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[18]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Fee
and Hubbard, <i>The Eerdmans Companion to the Bible</i>, 585.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn19" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref19" name="_ftn19" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn19;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[19]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Faithlife
Study Bible, via Logos Software</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">, Introduction to John, by John D.
Barry et al.,.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn20" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref20" name="_ftn20" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn20;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[20]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Jamieson,
Fausset, and Brown, <i>Commentary Critical and Explanatory on the Whole Bible
(1871)</i>, 126–27.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn21" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref21" name="_ftn21" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn21;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[21]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Darrell
Wolfe, “Class Assignment: Situational Reconstruction | Philemon” (Paper,
Biblical Background and Interpretation (2021FA-BIBL-2301-ONL), The King’s
University, Southlake, Texas, November 4, 2021),
http://www.darrellwolfe.com/2021/11/class-assignment-situational.html.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn22" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref22" name="_ftn22" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn22;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[22]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Walvoord,
Zuck, and Dallas Theological Seminary, <i>The Bible Knowledge Commentary</i>,
271;<span style="mso-spacerun: yes;">&nbsp;&nbsp; </span>The Prologue (1:1–18).</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn23" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref23" name="_ftn23" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn23;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[23]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Mounce,
<i>Mounce’s Complete Expository Dictionary of Old &amp; New Testament Words</i>.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn24" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref24" name="_ftn24" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn24;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[24]</span></span></span></span></a> <i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Lexham
Bible Dictionary</span></i><span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">, LOGOS, GREEK BACKGROUND.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn25" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref25" name="_ftn25" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn25;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[25]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Mounce,
<i>Mounce’s Complete Expository Dictionary of Old &amp; New Testament Words</i>,
801.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn26" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref26" name="_ftn26" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn26;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[26]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Wilhelm
Gesenius et al., <i>The Abridged Brown-Driver-Briggs Hebrew-English Lexicon of
the Old Testament: From A Hebrew and English Lexicon of the Old Testament.</i>
(Boston; New York: Houghton, Mifflin and Company, 1906).</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn27" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref27" name="_ftn27" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn27;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[27]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Tim
Mackie and Jon Collins, “God’s Word, Spirit, &amp; Wisdom Podcast |
BibleProject<sup>TM</sup>,” BibleProject, accessed November 26, 2021,
https://bibleproject.com/podcast/theme-god-e11-gods-word-spirit-wisdom/.</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn28" style="mso-element: footnote;">

<p class="MsoFootnoteText" style="text-indent: .5in;"><a href="#_ftnref28" name="_ftn28" style="mso-footnote-id: ftn28;" title=""><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="mso-special-character: footnote;"><span class="MsoFootnoteReference"><span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;,serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 107%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: AR-SA; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast;">[28]</span></span></span></span></a> <span style="mso-bidi-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-bidi-font-size: 12.0pt;">Gore,
Goudge, and Guillaume, <i>A NEW COMMENTARY ON HOLY SCRIPTURE INCLUDING THE
APOCRYPHA</i>, W. Lock, “The Gospel according to St. John,”<span style="mso-spacerun: yes;">&nbsp; </span>((Apostle Andrew (Muratorian Canon))).</span></p>

</div>

<div id="ftn29" style="mso-element: footnote;">
